section.title: The second derivative test
section.p: showed what replaces f'' in several variables. Near a critical point, f(\mathbf p+\mathbf u)-f(\mathbf p) is controlled first by \frac12\mathbf u^T H_f(\mathbf p)\mathbf u. So the question becomes a question about a quadratic expression.

p.md: \text{What signs can } \mathbf u^T H\mathbf u \text{ have?}

subsection.title: Reading the quadratic part
subsection.p: Consider the function B(x,y)=12+2x^2+xy+y^2. At the origin, B_x=4x+y,\qquad B_y=x+2y, so \nabla B(0,0)=\mathbf 0. The origin is a critical point.

The change from the origin is B(x,y)-B(0,0)=2x^2+xy+y^2. Complete the square: 2x^2+xy+y^2=2\left(x+\frac14y\right)^2+\frac78y^2. This is positive unless x=y=0. So B(x,y)\gt B(0,0) for every nearby nonzero point. The origin is a local minimum.
Now consider M(x,y)=12-2x^2-xy-y^2. Again the origin is a critical point, but M(x,y)-M(0,0)=-(2x^2+xy+y^2). So every nearby nonzero point gives a smaller value. The origin is a local maximum.

Finally consider S(x,y)=12+x^2-y^2. At the origin, 
abla S(0,0)=\mathbf 0. But S(x,0)=12+x^2\gt 12 for x
e0, while S(0,y)=12-y^2\lt 12 for y
e0. The origin is a saddle point.
The three quadratic parts behave differently:

table.title: Sign behavior of three quadratic parts.

row.cell: positive in some directions, negative in others

statement.p: Let H be a symmetric matrix, and define q(\mathbf u)=\mathbf u^T H\mathbf u.
The quadratic form q is called positive definite if q(\mathbf u)\gt 0 for every nonzero vector \mathbf u.
It is called negative definite if q(\mathbf u)\lt 0 for every nonzero vector \mathbf u.
It is called indefinite if it takes both positive and negative values.

subsection.p: The Hessian tells the local shape through its quadratic form.

figure.caption: A definite quadratic form has one sign in every nonzero direction. An indefinite form has both signs.

subsection.title: The two-variable determinant shortcut
subsection.p: For a two-variable function, the Hessian at a point has the form H=\begin{pmatrix}A \amp B\\B \amp C\end{pmatrix}. Its quadratic form is q(u,v)=\begin{pmatrix}u \amp v\end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix}A \amp B\\B \amp C\end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix}u\\v\end{pmatrix}=Au^2+2Buv+Cv^2. The number \boxed{\Delta=AC-B^2} decides most cases.

If A\ne0, complete the square:
q(u,v)\amp=Au^2+2Buv+Cv^2 \amp=A\left(u+\frac{B}{A}v\right)^2+\left(C-\frac{B^2}{A}\right)v^2 \amp=A\left(u+\frac{B}{A}v\right)^2+\frac{\Delta}{A}v^2.
Now the sign is visible.
The last case is the one where the quadratic part has a flat direction or disappears entirely. Then second order is not enough.

subsection.title: The second derivative test
subsection.p: Now put the quadratic-form reading together with the second-order approximation.

theorem.title: Second derivative test in two variables

statement.p: Let f(x,y) have continuous second partial derivatives near a point (a,b). Suppose (a,b) is a critical point: f_x(a,b)=0,\qquad f_y(a,b)=0. Set A=f_{xx}(a,b),\qquad B=f_{xy}(a,b),\qquad C=f_{yy}(a,b), and \Delta=AC-B^2.
Then:

proof.title: Proof idea
proof.p: At a critical point, the second-order approximation says f((a,b)+\mathbf u)-f(a,b)=\frac12\mathbf u^T H_f(a,b)\mathbf u+R(\mathbf u), where \frac{R(\mathbf u)}{\|\mathbf u\|^2}\to0.

If the quadratic form is positive definite, then it is positive in every nonzero direction, and the small remainder cannot change that sign when \mathbf u is close enough to \mathbf 0. So f has a local minimum.
If the quadratic form is negative definite, the same argument gives a local maximum.

If the quadratic form is indefinite, there is one direction where the quadratic part is positive and another direction where it is negative. Along sufficiently small motions in those two directions, the function takes values both above and below f(a,b). That is a saddle point.

subsection.p: The test is a sign test for the first nonzero visible part of the function.

example.title: A minimum and a saddle from the same function

statement.p: Let f(x,y)=x^3-3x+y^2. First find the critical points: f_x=3x^2-3,\qquad f_y=2y. Setting both equal to zero gives x=\pm1,\qquad y=0. So the critical points are (1,0)\qquad\text{and}\qquad(-1,0).
The second partials are f_{xx}=6x,\qquad f_{xy}=0,\qquad f_{yy}=2.
At (1,0), A=6,\qquad B=0,\qquad C=2. Thus \Delta=AC-B^2=12\gt 0, and A\gt 0. So (1,0) is a local minimum.
At (-1,0), A=-6,\qquad B=0,\qquad C=2. Thus \Delta=(-6)(2)-0=-12\lt 0. So (-1,0) is a saddle point.

For h(x,y)=100+4x+3y-x^2-xy-y^2, the critical point is P=\left(\frac53,\frac23\right). The second partials are h_{xx}=-2,\qquad h_{xy}=-1,\qquad h_{yy}=-2. So A=-2,\qquad B=-1,\qquad C=-2. Then \Delta=AC-B^2=(-2)(-2)-(-1)^2=4-1=3. Since \Delta\gt 0\qquad\text{and}\qquad A\lt 0, the Hessian is negative definite. Therefore \left(\frac53,\frac23\right) is a local maximum.
This matches the exact expansion h\left(\frac53+u,\frac23+v\right)=\frac{313}{3}-(u^2+uv+v^2).
Let s(x,y)=xy. Then s_x=y,\qquad s_y=x. The origin is a critical point.
The second partials are s_{xx}=0,\qquad s_{xy}=1,\qquad s_{yy}=0. So A=0,\qquad B=1,\qquad C=0. Then \Delta=AC-B^2=0-1=-1\lt 0. Therefore the origin is a saddle point.
The geometry is easy to see: s(t,t)=t^2\gt 0, but s(t,-t)=-t^2\lt 0. The function rises along one diagonal and falls along the other.

subsection.title: Why the critical-point condition is needed
subsection.p: A positive definite Hessian does not by itself mean minimum. The first-order part must be gone.
Consider g(x,y)=x+x^2+y^2. At the origin, H_g(0,0)=\begin{pmatrix}2 \amp 0\\0 \amp 2\end{pmatrix}, which is positive definite.
But the origin is not a critical point: \nabla g(0,0)=(1,0). In fact, g(-t,0)=-t+t^2\lt 0=g(0,0) for small positive t. So the origin is not a local minimum.
The Hessian tells us how the function bends. If the function is still sloping, the slope can dominate the bending.

subsection.title: Why the continuity condition is needed
subsection.p: The second derivative test also needs the second-order approximation to be valid. A Hessian computed at one point can mislead us if nearby second derivatives behave badly.

Define \Phi(x,y)=\begin{cases}x^2+y^2-8\dfrac{x^2y^2}{x^2+y^2}, \amp (x,y)\ne(0,0),\\[3mm]0, \amp (x,y)=(0,0).\end{cases} At the origin, \nabla \Phi(0,0)=\mathbf 0. Along the coordinate axes, \Phi(x,0)=x^2,\qquad \Phi(0,y)=y^2. A direct calculation gives \Phi_{xx}(0,0)=2,\qquad \Phi_{xy}(0,0)=0,\qquad \Phi_{yx}(0,0)=0,\qquad \Phi_{yy}(0,0)=2. So the Hessian at the origin is H_\Phi(0,0)=\begin{pmatrix}2 \amp 0\\0 \amp 2\end{pmatrix}, which is positive definite.

If the second derivative test applied, it would predict a local minimum. But along the line y=x=t, we get
\Phi(t,t)\amp=t^2+t^2-8\frac{t^4}{2t^2} \amp=2t^2-4t^2 \amp=-2t^2.
So \Phi(t,t)\lt \Phi(0,0) for every nonzero t close to 0. The origin is not a local minimum.

The second partials exist at the origin, but they are not continuous near the origin. The quadratic approximation is not trustworthy here. The continuity condition in the test protects us from this kind of behavior.

subsection.title: Degenerate Hessians
subsection.p: If \Delta=0, the second derivative test gives no conclusion. This does not mean the point is unusual or impossible to classify. It means the quadratic part does not decide.

statement.p: Let f(x,y)=x^4+y^4. At the origin, \nabla f(0,0)=\mathbf 0. The Hessian is H_f(0,0)=\begin{pmatrix}0 \amp 0\\0 \amp 0\end{pmatrix}. So \Delta=0. The second derivative test says nothing.
But the origin is clearly a local minimum, because x^4+y^4\ge0, with equality only at (0,0). Here the first nonzero part is fourth order, not second order.

example.title: The same degenerate Hessian with a saddle

statement.p: Let g(x,y)=x^4-y^4. Again \nabla g(0,0)=\mathbf 0, and again H_g(0,0)=\begin{pmatrix}0 \amp 0\\0 \amp 0\end{pmatrix}. The Hessian is exactly the same as in the previous example.
But now g(t,0)=t^4\gt 0, while g(0,t)=-t^4\lt 0. So the origin is a saddle point.
The same Hessian produced a minimum in one function and a saddle in another. A degenerate Hessian does not carry enough information.

subsection.p: When the test is degenerate, we look for another idea: higher-order terms, special factoring, restrictions to curves, or a direct inequality.

subsection.title: A practical checklist
subsection.p: For an interior optimization problem with a twice continuously differentiable function of two variables:

row.cell: Find critical points by solving f_x=0,\ f_y=0.
Use the sign of \Delta and A to classify when possible.

subsection.p: This checklist is for interior critical points. It does not check boundaries. It also does not handle constraints.

That limitation matters. A marble rolling on a circular track wrapped around a hill cannot move in every direction. The ordinary gradient may not be zero at the highest point on the track, because the steepest uphill direction may point off the track. To optimize when motion is restricted, we need a new first-order condition. It will look strange at first: \nabla f=\lambda \nabla g. The number \lambda is a Lagrange multiplier.
